title: Tachycardia and hypotension after surgery
summary: Early recognition of sepsis vs hypovolemia using a focused assessment and safe escalation.
vignette: A 68-year-old post-op day 2 has HR 118, BP 102/58, RR 22, T 38.6°C, and SpO₂ 94% on RA. They report fatigue and mild confusion. Urine output has been 15 mL/hr for two hours.
question: Which action best matches initial stabilization and scope?
options: Activate rapid response / provider notification, obtain IV access if needed, begin ordered fluids/antibiotics per protocol, and reassess frequently.
Encourage oral fluids only and recheck in 4 hours.
Discharge home with follow-up since surgery was uncomplicated.
takeaway: Unexplained hypotension with infection concern needs timely escalation, perfusion-focused care, and repeat assessment—not watchful waiting.

title: Peaked T waves and weakness
summary: Linking ECG changes and labs when potassium may be critically high.
vignette: A patient with CKD reports generalized weakness. ECG shows peaked T waves. Stat potassium is pending.
question: While awaiting the lab, what is the most appropriate immediate priority?
options: Follow institutional protocols for suspected hyperkalemia stabilization (including cardiac monitoring and ordered therapies) and notify the provider.
Send the patient to the waiting room to reduce hallway noise.
Hold all fluids because potassium might be high.
takeaway: Suspected symptomatic hyperkalemia is treated as an emergency; use protocol-driven stabilization and continuous monitoring.
